speaker: interviewer
text: July 21, 1996. Freddy Diament, Dana-- The date is July 21, 1996. Survivor, Freddy Diament. Interviewer, Dana Schwartz. City, Studio City. State, California. Country, USA. Language, English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Dana Schwartz. I'm here with Freddy Diament. We're in Studio City, California. We'll be speaking English. And today is July 21, 1996. This is Dana Schwartz. I'm sitting here, again, with Freddy Diament. Freddy, what is your full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freddy Ephraim Diament.

speaker: interviewer
text: What name were you born with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freddy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ephraim?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Ephraim is my Hebrew name but I used it as a middle name later in life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Gelsenkirchen, in Germany. It is located in the Ruhr valley, the industrial belt of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: March the 30th, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about your grandparents? Do-- or as far back as you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know only what I was told by my mother and my father. Personally, I did not know them. They lived in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both sides.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your mother's family names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Weiss.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the first names? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about her mother and her father's names? Do you know them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her father's name was Saul. Saul. That I know. Her mother's name, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where they lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They lived in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Krakow. In [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: In [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a Jewish name for it. [YIDDISH], they called it, in Yiddish. Not far from Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know anything about them at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. What-- what my mother told us about them. I know that he was a merchant. And I also knew that he got-- besides he was a-- a Talmudic scholar. I also know that he spoke German. And my mother was, every-- every time, she was so proud. When the Austrians in the First World War entered the city, he wrote the statute in German. He was educated enough that he could write it down for the occupying forces. Because they chased away the Russians, the Austrian army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. She had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: More or less. I know one-- the youngest one was Josef, also Weiss. Then they had a sister, was Hilda Weiss. And she had an older brother. His name was [INAUDIBLE] Weiss. He lived in Berlin before the war. And then she had the oldest sister, [? Helen, ?] lived in the United States, in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of your cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she-- wait a minute. She had another brother. One was [? Nathan, ?] and he lived also in Germany. And then she had a younger brother, [? Shmuel, ?] who went in 1923 to Israel. He was a-- a pioneer [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So did any of the offspring of those people survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Those in Israel, for sure, survived. But those in-- in-- in-- in the occupied territories, none survived. To the best of my knowledge. I did some research. I couldn't find anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: To go to your father's side now, what were your father's parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, my name is after my grandfather. His father's name was Ephraim. And his mother's name was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also in Poland. They lived in a small town, also not far from Krakow, called Lancut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what it was called-- did it have another name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Lancut. As a matter of fact, they've found now the best-maintained synagogue, that was built in the 15th century. And it's very famous. It was all restored and became almost like a museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell the name of that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lancut? L-A-N-C-- with a little-- underneath-- U-T. Lancut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. As a matter of fact, the-- the city is very known that the famous Graf Potocki had a giant liquor factory in the city. That's what the city is famous for. And to the best of my knowledge, it still-- the factory still exists.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did Ephraim do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he was also-- he had-- I think that he had a grocery store. Yeah. He also was-- was a-- a [NON-ENGLISH]. He was very much involve in-- in-- in-- in studying Torah. And to the best of my knowledge, his wife ran the business more than he did. He was studying day and night, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have any other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They had a lot of children. I think eight or nine. I remember the-- the names of the brothers. One was Yaakov. And he lived in [POLISH], also a city, probably 50 miles north of-- of-- of Lancut. And the other name was David. And some of his children lived in Israel. I knew them. And he lived in a-- in a city, very well-known city-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- called [NON-ENGLISH]. Also not far from Krakow. They all lived in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Then he had a sister, Bertha, who lived in [NON-ENGLISH] in-- in-- in-- in [NON-ENGLISH], not far from my hometown, in Germany. Who else is there? Let me see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then he had a-- a-- a sister, Ida. As a matter of fact, the interesting thing is she was married to a man. They were very poor. And they, when the Nazis occupied Poland, fled to Russia. And they had nine children. And all survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are--

speaker: interviewee
text: And now--

speaker: interviewer
text: --they now?

speaker: interviewee
text: --They live in-- in Brooklyn. She all-- they all have great grandchildren. And they are all Chabad Hasidim. Lubavitcher Hasidim. Very active. Very, very active. They must be-- the family must be today with children, great-- grandchildren, great-grandchildren, at least a hundred. It's amazing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they're Diaments.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She was a Diament. Her husband was-- the name was Friedman.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. It was the sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of the other cousins-- your cousins survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I told you that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Other than Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel, that's all. Yeah. And there were plenty. I know that-- that-- that-- the-- the oldest brother, Yaakov, had 10 children. My father used to tell me that. And I couldn't find anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did a search?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I go to the committee in Poland and-- and-- and-- and even right after the war, I passed by in those towns. Couldn't find anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names? What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Simon. Shimon in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother's name was-- was Amalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amalia. And what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, interesting enough, until the age of 28, he never worked a day. He was a [NON-ENGLISH]. He was really, in the true sense of the word, a Talmudic scholar. He-- he knew whole pages by heart, even then. And then, when they came to Germany in 1919, and they married, also, right away, in-- in-- they met in Poland, he and my mother. And then they married in-- in Duisberg. It's also a city, between Dusseldorf and Gelsenkirchen. And then he went into business and he opened up a textile store in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how your parents met?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot. Honestly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think--

speaker: interviewee
text: But they met-- they met-- they-- they were introduced. What you call a Shidduch.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Shidduch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they married. And were you their first child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. I think they were introduced through a brother of my mother. Now I remember. [? Nathan. ?] He introduced him. I think they were together in-- in-- in the army. Now I remember that. Yeah. The both were in the Austrian Army in the First World War. They were more or less the same age. And he introduced-- [? Nathan ?] introduced his sister to my father. That's the way it was. Now I remember it. And-- and [? Nathan ?] survived the war, also in Israel-- in Palestine, so to speak. And he told me a story. Now I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your parents got married, you say, in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Without the parents, just by themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: Their parents were-- were-- were dead already. They have died. Yeah. They died at a relatively young age. In the late 50s they died. The father and the mother. I think that-- they died in the First World War. There must have been an epidemic or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your parents now. What they did, what they were like--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Let me say this. My parents were-- my father, particularly, were-- was a modern Orthodox Jew. And they were [NON-ENGLISH] Shabbat. They-- and we were one of the very few families in our town, in Gelsenkirchen, where the business-- the store-- was close on the Shabbat. Although it was a major business day, they really lived by what they pretended to-- to be. And my father was very active in the Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, pretended to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people, they are-- they are very religious. But when it comes to money, they compromise. So he lived by what he-- what he-- what he believed in, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was not the first one. I was the third child. We were three boys that were born one after the other, with a year difference. And I remember very well how proud he was when he went to the shul with the three boys. Like a [INAUDIBLE] you know? Big, small, smaller.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got a very intensive Jewish education. As a matter of fact, first we went to cheder. But then, we had a private teacher, what they called in-- in-- in Yiddish, a melamed, that came to our house. It was very expensive. But my father insisted on this. And we got quite an intensive Jewish education-- at least for Germany. The-- the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], so-- the reason--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your brothers' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother name is Saul. And as a result of this, he became a rabbi later, in United States. My brother is-- the oldest brother is the only one that was not in the Shoah. He got out of Germany in the last minute, practically, in 1939, and went illegally to Israel, over [NON-ENGLISH]. And he arrive with a ship on the same day as the Second World War broke out, on September the 1st. And they were seized by-- the ship was seized by the British. And they just all jump into the sea and dynamited the ship. So. The-- the wreck of the ship is still in Tel Aviv harbor [INAUDIBLE]. And he arrived in Israel in his swim trunks. That's all he had. He was lucky that he had two uncles in-- in-- in Tel Aviv. The two younger brothers of my mother. They took them-- the youngest uncle took him like a-- almost adopted him. And he lived there with his uncle for-- for-- for two years. And then he joined a kibbutz. That's my brother. Then he came to United States later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what about the second-- the second brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was with me, together, and my father, in the concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leo. Leo Yehuda. Since he also was a member of the [NON-ENGLISH] group, he called himself the Hebrew name, Yehuda. And he was with me together for almost five years. And then he has executed. He was hanged by the Nazis in-- in-- in Auschwitz, because he was an active member of the underground. And he and two his friends-- they were also hanged-- were actively involve in preparing a mass outbreak.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'll tell me about that later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later. Later. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, after that, there were-- after you, there were no more children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was my sister. My sister, Elly. She went with my mother in-- let me put it this way. We were arrested in-- in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, don't tell me what happened during the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just tell me what happened before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. She-- she-- she was younger. She was two years, three years younger than me. She went to school. And then I had two smaller children. One is-- was named [? Sammy, ?] who also went to school. He was-- before the Holocaust, he was probably 12 years old. And then I had a very young brother, was only six years old. He was-- his name was Martin. So we were six children. Five boys and one girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about-- about your family life?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have quite a very good family life. My-- I forgot to mention before, my father also had a younger brother, [INAUDIBLE], who also lived in our town. I forgot to mention it. And he died in between now. And he first had in partnership with my father at the store. And then he got married, was introduced to my mother-- to-- to his wife. And then they split up. And he changed his name, interesting enough, to [? Diamant, ?] so that people don't get mixed up. And-- and they both were very active involve in the Jewish-- in the [GERMAN] community. There were two communities in Germany. The-- call it indigenous German Jews. They had their own synagogue. And the Jews that came from Poland, they called them [GERMAN], eastern Jews. So we belonged, of course, since my parents came from Poland, we belonged to the [GERMAN] community. And there was a very active-- and my father was all the time in the committee, in the-- in the [INAUDIBLE], they called this. And he also-- I remember once in awhile, he gave courses and taught other people the Talmud Gemara. And-- but I very much remember is, Shabbat was really Shabbat. Was a day of-- of pray, a day of resting. And it was almost a tradition in Germany, after the-- the-- the-- the davening, after the prayer, they went to the park. Each city had a beautiful park. And you could see only Jews in the park. Beautiful park with flowers, with-- with-- with-- with a little artificial lake.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we went home for dinner. And-- and-- and after dinner, my father used to give us always a lecture about the midrash. That was the most interesting thing. Because the midrash is really-- that tells the story of the Bible, more more-- let's put it this way-- in detail than the-- than the-- the Torah that you read, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was most interesting when he gave us-- midrash means, really, explanation. The [HEBREW] in Hebrew. That means to more explain the text of the Torah. And-- and that-- that was so interesting. And sometimes we said more, more, more. Although we were-- we-- we are learning all year round in cheder, and then when the-- the teacher came to our house. But the midrash was very interesting. Was really a-- quite a-- let's put it this way-- a treat. That's the word. Was quite a treat. My parents were observing Jews. We were strictly kosher in the house. We celebrate all the Jewish holidays, the way it should be. And as I said, we got a very intensive Jewish education. All the children. Including my-- my-- my sister. But not from the-- from the melamed. Not from the teacher. She went to a-- to a day school there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to the park, did you go with your mother, or without your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, yeah. The whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, would you sit, or would you walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were walking, and sitting, and meeting other friends. It was-- but it was a meeting place, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have lunch in the park?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. No. We ate at home. No. As I said, my parents wouldn't eat anywhere. I-- I don't think we had a kosher restaurant in our city. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother-- did your mother go to the shul with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very often. We met her in the park, as a matter of fact. She went on holidays. But not every Shabbat. My father went every Shabbat, and even every day, sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Friday nights you had a Shabbat dinner.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- tell me-- tell me the fun stuff. Did you have fun with your brothers and sister? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, are you kidding me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- yeah, tell us. Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were fighting day and night. We-- we had-- in our house, it was-- always something was going on. Because even the little ones, when there are five boys-- we were fanatic soccer players. And we always pretended we had our own team. Because there's three, and then we-- we had friends. And they're constantly-- you know, in-- in-- in-- at that time in Germany, everybody played soccer, even in the street. And we lived in an area where there was very little traffic. So-- in-- in a very, very beautiful home we had there. And then we were playing soccer. And-- and-- belonged-- we belonged, also, to a Jewish sports club, the Hakoah. We didn't-- there was-- there were two competing sports club-- Maccabi and Hakoah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many balls did you have at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my god. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Soccer balls. I mean, did you have more than one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no fighting over the ball.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the fighting about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I did most of the fighting. Because my brother, the oldest brother, wanted to be the boss all the time. And I didn't accept that. I said, wait a minute. Wait a minute. There is no birth right that you have [INAUDIBLE]. And so there were arguments going on. You know. But-- but in-- in general, we stuck together, let's put it this way. Obviously.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your-- the second brother? Did-- Did he get into the fights, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the peacemaker. He was the peacemaker. The second brother was always the peacemaker. And if something was going on, I remember his-- he was such an altruist, that's the word. When-- when we did something wrong, and my parent want to know who did it, when something was broken, he volunteered always. He volunteered. He was such an idealist. Yeah. And all his life, he was an idealist. That's probably the reason that he got in trouble, and-- and-- and didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have much to do with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I-- interesting enough, she complains even now. I was her boss. Everybody had a boss. I commandeered her around. Because I was the older brother. The two oldest brothers were very close. And-- and-- and then-- there are always cliques when you have a lot of children. And I was close to my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I helped her. I went to high school. She went to high school. To do gymnasium. She went to lyceum.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I remember she had problems with Latin. I helped her a little bit. I was-- I was a good student, so-- there was a relationship, very close. And-- although I disciplined her, so to speak. Because I was the older brother. But I stuck up for her and she stuck up for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you guys hit each other ever?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father ever hit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can remember only-- he-- he caught me smoking when I was 12 years old. That's the only time he hit me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about--

speaker: interviewee
text: With a--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother? With what? What-- you said with--

speaker: interviewee
text: With a stick. With [GERMAN]. I mean, it didn't hurt, really. So, but what-- so to speak, symbolically. He wanted to, to make it clear to me that this is not acceptable, period.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother never hit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was she like as a mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a very devoted mother. But she also was very active in the business. See, my father was not a businessman. My mother was the-- I was told by my mother when she was 16 years old, she moved to Vienna. I don't know if you know that, that they say, 80% of the Viennese were Galaciano, practically. Their parents all came from Galicia and they moved to Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he moved to Vienna with a friend and opened up a textile store, for-- for-- for fashion textiles, so to speak. She liked fashion very-- as a young girl. And-- and she had the-- the experience. She was a born businesswoman. It was in her blood, so to speak. She was very good at it. And having six children and being active in the store was very interesting. And she found the time. Of course, at that time, we did pretty good. We had a maid and a governant. Governess? Governess.

speaker: interviewee
text: A governess. For the children. Because she-- somebody had to take care of the children. Especially the little ones, you know. So she could find the time to be in-- in-- in-- in the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she strict or loving? What would you characterize--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was-- she was loving. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was loving.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Even my father was not that strict.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school-- you said you went to a gymnasium.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- all children went. All the three brothers. The oldest, the second, and me went to gymnasium. We were at-- at the age already. And my sister, Elly, went to lyceum. To the best of my knowledge, she was only 10 years-- she went, 2 or 2 and 1/2 years she went. Until 1938, where we all were thrown out of school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we were allowed to go to-- to the-- to high school, which was unusual, because we were considered Polish citizens. If we would have been Germans, they would have thrown us out earlier. They-- for some reason, that is not very logical, the Nazis, in order to maintain good relations with foreign countries, allowed Jews to go to public high schools if they were foreign citizens. It's interesting. If we would-- the German Jews-- I remember, I had in my class, two boys-- they were German Jews-- in 1936, after the Nuremberg Laws came out, after the Olympics, they were thrown out of school. They were not allowed, anymore, to school. And-- so I was Jewish, but I was considered a foreign citizen-- or my parents were foreign citizens. They allowed us to stay in school until Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my god, and how. It was a frightening experience. That really-- the Kristallnacht-- was, so to speak, I would say, the general rehearsal of the Nazis for the Holocaust. It really was. Because until that night, although there were boycotts, there were individual persecution of-- of-- of individuals, there were the Nuremberg Law, that deprived the Jews of making a living, throwing them out from all their professions. They were not allowed to work in-- in-- in-- in-- in-- in-- in-- in universities. They were totally outcast. But they still could make a living. And they-- they were boycotting the stores with signs to don't buy from the Jews. The Jews are our-- our-- our-- our-- our-- [GERMAN]-- our misfortune, so to speak. But in Kristallnacht, [? it ?] was a very frightening experience. And-- and I remember on November the 9th, first of all, we saw the-- the synagogue was inside where we lived. We saw the synagogue burning. And we heard the noise and the screaming. Most of those, they were not SS. They were SA. [GERMAN], they called them. They were not as young. They were more of the elderly.

speaker: interviewer
text: [GERMAN]--

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. SR, they call it. SA in English. And some of them were literally drunk. And they went from store to store. And there were few other Jewish stores, not much further we lived. In Germany at that time, it was customary that you had a store on the ground floor and you lived upstairs. We lived in the fourth floor. It was a nice building. And I remember we heard the screaming. And-- and-- and it broke all the-- the-- the-- the big windows. And-- and by force, they-- they-- they broke the-- the-- the-- the iron door and-- and went-- and started destroying everything and burning everything. We smelled the-- the fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, the most frightening thing was, they were knocking at the door. There was a very strong door to the entrance to the apartments. And they scream all the time, are there Jews, more Jews, in that house?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they did in many instances, they beat up people. They were-- they were drunk. And the police did not interfere. The police had strict-- we found out after, two days later-- everybody, every Jew had one friend at the police. And they mostly informed of what was going on, even among the gestapo, interesting enough. There were Germans that somehow knew some of the Jewish citizens for years and years, at least informed him, watch it. There is an action being planned. Hide, don't be at home, what have you. Interesting enough, there were actions by the Nazis, by the Gestapo, when you were not at home, you were-- you were lucky. I give an example. If you remember-- let me tell you a story about the Kristallnacht. They were knocking at the door and wanted to get up. And fortunately enough, on the top floor, there was a very poor German family. My mother was very philanthropic, so to speak. Poor people, if they were Jewish, or Germans, she helped him. From the store, stuff that didn't sell, she'd give to-- [INAUDIBLE] she had six children. And one of her sons, I think it was the oldest son, became an SS man. SS man. And-- and-- and-- and he did not make nasty remarks to us. Because his mother didn't tolerate it. And we begged the mother, please, tell your son to go to the window and tell the-- the people downstairs there are no Jews here. And let him put on his SS uniform. She didn't want to do that. And my mother begged her and cried. Eventually, he did it. And he went to the window. And that really saved us from being hurt. I'll give you an example. There was a family about two blocks from us. They had a big store, a very big store, one of the biggest shoe stores. And they also lived on the second floor in the same house. They literally took the man and the woman and threw 'em out of the window. Just like that. As if they were garbage. And the man was totally paralyzed after this. The woman was sick and broke her leg or her arm. But the husband was paralyzed after this. That we heard after the event. And we-- but we were afraid that they will hurt us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Freddy, we're going to stop now to change the tape.
